query.query_text: pelvic mass in 75-year-old female; need memory about targeted history and exam before imaging decision; avoid premature diagnosis

positive_experience.situation: When a 75-year-old female presents with pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, and dyspareunia, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should update the hypothesis to consider malignancy as a default assumption.
positive_experience.action: Update the differential diagnosis to include malignancy as a default assumption, even in the absence of imaging or lab results, to avoid premature closure.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when facing a pelvic mass in an elderly woman with no imaging or lab results; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and confirm a benign diagnosis.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient reports pelvic pressure and constipation in an elderly woman, the doctor should consider the possibility of a pelvic mass compressing the rectum and ask about bowel habits to guide imaging.
positive_experience.action: Ask about bowel habits and constipation to identify potential mechanical compression from a pelvic mass and prioritize imaging.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when a patient reports pelvic pressure and constipation in an elderly woman; do not use if the patient has a clear history of benign fibroids or if imaging confirms a benign mass.

positive_experience.situation: When a patient reports heavy menstrual bleeding and fatigue in an elderly woman with pelvic symptoms, the doctor should ask targeted questions about bleeding patterns and associated symptoms to refine the differential.
positive_experience.action: Ask targeted questions about menstrual bleeding patterns, associated symptoms, and recent changes to identify potential causes like fibroids or anemia.
positive_experience.boundary: Use this memory when a patient reports heavy bleeding and fatigue in an elderly woman with pelvic symptoms; do not use if the patient has a clear history of benign fibroids or if imaging confirms a benign mass.

positive_experience.situation: When hip pain is described as aching and localized with stiffness and functional limitation, but no imaging or labs are available, what should the doctor avoid?
positive_experience.action: Avoid finalizing diagnosis without imaging or physical exam findings, as this may lead to premature closure and miss alternative diagnoses like infection or fracture.
positive_experience.boundary: Use when imaging or physical exam is missing; do not use if imaging is available and shows clear osteoarthritis or if the patient has systemic symptoms suggesting infection or malignancy.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, cramping, syncope, and ankle pain, especially in an elderly female, and initial labs are unavailable, the doctor should avoid premature diagnosis without imaging or targeted physical exam.
negative_experience.action: Request imaging of the abdomen and pelvis to evaluate for bowel obstruction or diverticulitis, and perform a focused physical exam including assessment of distension, tenderness, and pulses in the lower extremities.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when the patient has constipation followed by diarrhea with mucus, cramping, syncope, and ankle pain, and when labs are unavailable or inconclusive; do not use if imaging is contraindicated or if the patient has clear signs of fracture on physical exam or prior imaging.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with a pelvic mass and symptoms like pressure, urinary frequency, and bloating, but no imaging or lab results are available, and the differential is uncertain, avoid finalizing diagnosis without ruling out benign causes.
negative_experience.action: Request imaging and labs before finalizing diagnosis, especially when the patient is symptomatic and the mass is not clearly benign.
negative_experience.boundary: Use this memory when imaging and labs are missing and the patient has symptoms suggestive of a pelvic mass; do not use if imaging or labs are available and confirm benign findings.

negative_experience.situation: When a patient presents with non-specific symptoms and no clear diagnosis, and initial targeted history and lab/imaging requests yield no findings, the doctor should avoid premature finalization without ruling out coagulation disorders.
negative_experience.action: Delay final diagnosis until coagulation labs or imaging are reviewed, or until a targeted question about bleeding or bruising is asked.
negative_experience.boundary: Use when patient has non-specific symptoms and no clear diagnosis, but only if coagulation labs or bleeding history are not yet reviewed; do not use if bleeding or bruising history is already known or if labs/imaging are unavailable.

content.situation: When a 75-year-old female presents with pelvic pressure, bloating, urinary frequency, and dyspareunia, and initial labs and imaging are unavailable, the doctor should update the hypothesis to consider malignancy as a default assumption.
content.action: Update the differential diagnosis to include malignancy as a default assumption, even in the absence of imaging or lab results, to avoid premature closure.
content.outcome: This action prevented premature finalization and ensured that malignancy was considered, aligning with best practices for pelvic masses in elderly women.
content.boundary: Use this memory when facing a pelvic mass in an elderly woman with no imaging or lab results; do not use if imaging or lab results are available and confirm a benign diagnosis.

arguments.question: Have you experienced any specific symptoms such as pelvic pain, bloating, changes in bowel or bladder habits, or unexplained weight loss?

clinical_turn.patient_or_tool_response: {'question': 'I’ve had pelvic pain sometimes, especially when I’m having my period. I also feel bloated a lot, and I’ve noticed I need to go to the bathroom more often, especially at night. I’ve had some trouble with constipation too, and my periods have been heavier than before — I’ve had to start taking iron supplements because I’m often tired. I’ve also had pain during sex. I haven’t lost weight, but I’ve noticed my belly has gotten bigger, even though I’m not pregnant.'}

case_memory.chief_complaint: direct admission for evaluation of pelvic mass
current_turn_information: I’ve had pelvic pain sometimes, especially when I’m having my period.
I also feel bloated a lot, and I’ve noticed I need to go to the bathroom more often, especially at night.
I’ve had some trouble with constipation too, and my periods have been heavier than before — I’ve had to start taking iron supplements because I’m often tired.
I’ve also had pain during sex.
I haven’t lost weight, but I’ve noticed my belly has gotten bigger, even though I’m not pregnant.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 75-year-old female, direct admission for evaluation of pelvic mass. History of mechanical MVR and CAD s/p 1V CABG. Outpatient workup revealed spinal mass at T-12/L1 suspicious for metastasis with epidural mass and cord compression. Currently asymptomatic except for back pain managed with TENS, Vicodin, and Skelaxin. Denies B/B incontinence, constipation, focal weakness, or saddle anesthesia. Endorses L calf tightness and occasional tingling in L foot. Denies worsening fatigue, nausea/vomiting, or abdominal fullness. States possible 2 lb weight loss. Past medical history includes CAD, prosthetic mitral valve, TIA, low back pain, migraines, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GI bleed, osteoporosis. Family history: strokes in grandmother and mother. Physical exam: well-appearing, slightly pale, no obvious pain. Neuro exam: intact CN, normal strength, sensation, no saddle anesthesia. Final result: Intramural leiomyoma of uterus. Knowledge base: Uterine fibroid diagnosis, signs/symptoms, prognosis, and imaging.

Endorses L calf tightness and occasional tingling in L foot. Denies worsening fatigue, nausea/vomiting, abdominal fullness. May have lost 2 lbs. Past medical history: CAD, prosthetic mitral valve, TIA, low back pain, migraines, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GI bleed, osteoporosis. Family history: strokes in grandmother and mother. Physical exam: well-appearing, slightly pale, no obvious pain. Neuro: intact CN, normal strength, sensation, no saddle anesthesia. Final result: Intramural leiomyoma of uterus. Knowledge base: Uterine fibroid diagnosis, signs/symptoms, prognosis.

case_memory.chief_complaint: direct admission for evaluation of pelvic mass
current_turn_information: I’ve had pelvic pain sometimes, especially when I’m having my period. I also feel bloated a lot, and I’ve noticed I need to go to the bathroom more often, especially at night. I’ve had some trouble with constipation too, and my periods have been heavier than before — I’ve had to start taking iron supplements because I’m often tired. I’ve also had pain during sex. I haven’t lost weight, but I’ve noticed my belly has gotten bigger, even though I’m not pregnant.

Yes, I’ve noticed my periods have been heavier than usual lately, and I’ve had to change pads more often. I’ve also been feeling more tired than normal, which I think might be from losing too much blood. I haven’t had any bleeding between periods, but I do get some lower back pain sometimes, especially around my period.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 75-year-old female, direct admission for evaluation of pelvic mass. History of mechanical MVR and CAD s/p 1V CABG. Outpatient workup revealed spinal mass at T-12/L1 suspicious for metastasis with bony involvement and epidural mass causing cord compression. Patient denies B/B incontinence, constipation, focal weakness, saddle anesthesia. Endorses L calf tightness and occasional tingling in L foot. Denies worsening fatigue, nausea/vomiting, abdominal fullness. States possible 2 lb weight loss. Past medical history includes CAD, prosthetic mitral valve, TIA, low back pain, migraines, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GI bleed, osteoporosis. Family history: strokes in grandmother and mother. Physical exam: VS 97.6, 142/78, 111, 16, 98% RA. Well-appearing, slightly pale, no obvious pain. Neuro: intact CN, normal strength, sensation, no saddle anesthesia. Final result: Intramural leiomyoma of uterus. Knowledge base: Uterine fibroid (benign smooth muscle tumor), signs/symptoms include pelvic pain, bloating, urinary frequency, constipation, heavy bleeding, dyspareunia. Diagnosis via ultrasound/MRI. Prognosis: rare malignancy (leiomyosarcoma).

case_memory.chief_complaint: direct admission for evaluation of pelvic mass
current_turn_information: No recent trauma or surgeries.
Changes in bladder habits: needing to go more often, even when not drinking much.
Lower back pain and discomfort during sex.
Pressure in rectal area lately.
Document 1: History and Symptoms - lower abdominal/pelvic pain with pressure, vaginal bleeding, dyspareunia, bloating, abdominal distension, urinary symptoms raise suspicion of malignancy.
Document 2: Pelvic masses - most commonly found in women, consider every case as malignancy, likelihood increases with age.
Document 3: Imaging - pelvic ultrasound for masses in uterus to determine existence, size & composition.
Document 4: Non-surgical management of pelvic organ prolapse.
Document 5: Imaging - do not use MRI, CT, X-ray for routine assessment of urinary incontinence; ultrasound only for residual urine volume.
case_memory.prior_information_summary: 75-year-old female, direct admission for evaluation of pelvic mass. History of mechanical MVR and CAD s/p 1V CABG. Outpatient workup revealed spinal mass and newly found pelvic mass. Pain located midline lower back, radiating to legs. Uses TENS unit and Vicodin/Skelaxin for back pain. Bone lesion at T-12/L1 suspicious for metastasis, epidural mass with cord pressure. Denies B/B incontinence, constipation, focal weakness, saddle anesthesia. Endorses L calf tightness and occasional tingling in L foot. Denies worsening fatigue, nausea/vomiting, abdominal fullness. May have lost 2 lbs. Past medical history: CAD, prosthetic mitral valve, TIA, low back pain, migraines, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GI bleed, osteoporosis. Social history: family history of strokes. Physical exam: VS 97.6, 142/78, 111, 16, 98% RA. Well-appearing, slightly pale, no obvious pain. Neuro: intact CN, normal strength, sensation, no saddle anesthesia. Final result: Intramural leiomyoma of uterus. Knowledge base: Uterine fibroid - benign smooth muscle tumor, common in women 50+, symptoms include pelvic pain, heavy bleeding, pressure on bladder, pain during sex, back pain. Diagnosis: ultrasound/MRI sufficient. Prognosis: rare malignancy (leiomyosarcoma), growth after menopause raises concern. Coexisting disorders: anemia, constipation, hydronephrosis possible. Treatment: NSAIDs, iron supplements, surgery if symptomatic. Patient reports pelvic pain, bloating, constipation, frequent urination, fatigue, dyspareunia, lower back pain. Labs and imaging results not found in record.
